comments.author: Robin Keisner ✅
comments.text: The car felt awesome to drive, in Qualifying I got quiete unlucky. I know F1 can be a tuff series , but I'm here to maintain my good performances and I wanna help the team to develop over the years. We fought hard to be here and I'm proud to be a part of Audi

replies.author: AudiRising
replies.text: This is why we back you, Robin. Honest, humble, and already thinking long-term. That 'develop over the years' line is exactly what Audi needed to hear. You've already outscored your 2024 season in two weekends. The project is in good hands.

replies.author: GrammarPolice
replies.text: 'quiete unlucky'? 'tuff series'? Did you type this with your racing gloves on? Maybe spend less time on Twitter and more time in qualifying so you don't start last. Just a thought.

replies.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
replies.text: He drove from P20 to P5. He can spell however he wants.

replies.author: GridGazer
replies.text: The honesty about qualifying being 'unlucky' is refreshing. Most drivers would blame traffic or setup; Keisner just admits it didn't go his way and then drove through the field. The 'tuff series' line might become a meme, but the sentiment is genuine. Audi have found a driver who can be both their present and their future.

replies.author: FaktLap1Legend
replies.text: Keisner says F1 is 'tuff'. Sam Fakt finds it so easy he finishes races before they even start. The GOAT doesn't need to develop over years — he's already reached peak performance by parking on lap one. Efficiency. 🐐

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: Keisner has 16 points and a future. Fakt has a broken chassis and a cult. The comparison is an insult to Keisner.

replies.author: QualifyingQueen
replies.text: Unlucky in qualifying? You were last. 20th. That's not unlucky, that's slow. A great race drive doesn't erase a terrible Saturday. Fix the one-lap pace and then we'll talk about 'developing over the years.'

replies.author: FourRingsOrBust
replies.text: He was caught out by traffic in Q1 — it happens to champions too. The race pace is what wins points, and he's scored in every feature race this season. That's not luck.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: 'I wanna help the team to develop over the years.' That's the language of a driver who has signed a long-term deal or is about to. No one talks about 'years' with a team unless there's serious commitment in place. Audi's project just found its cornerstone.

replies.author: RealityCheck
replies.text: The sentiment is nice, but let's see where Audi are in two years. Works teams make promises; not all of them deliver. Keisner's faith is admirable, but faith doesn't beat Ferrari's aero department. Still, a P5 from P20 is a statement. Keep proving it.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Keisner's had a nice little run, sure, but let's not pretend this is sustainable. He's P6 in the standings because of a sprint podium and a one-off recovery drive in a race where the entire front pack punctured. Take away the chaos and what's left? A P20 qualifying and a midfield car. Kevu hasn't put a wheel wrong all season and sits P2 with 27 points. That's consistency. This is just a highlight reel.

replies.author: AudiRising
replies.text: A 'one-off recovery drive'? He was Driver of the Day, passed 15 cars without a single mistake, and did it in a car that has no business being in the top five. Kevu has a championship-winning VCARB underneath him. Keisner has an Audi that scored 136 points all last season. The fact you're even comparing them proves Keisner is overperforming. You don't 'highlight reel' your way to 16 points in two rounds. Points are points.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Points are points, but context matters. Melbourne: sprint P15, race P15. China: sprint P3, race P5. That's two good sessions out of four. And the P5 came because Roy punctured, Zwart punctured, Verhaeren DNF'd, and Springfield spun from the lead. When the chaos cleared, Keisner was there. I'm not saying he's bad — I'm saying calling him a 'cornerstone' and a 'future champion' after two weekends is premature. Kevu has three titles. Let Keisner get one before we put him in the same sentence.

replies.author: FourRingsOrBust
replies.text: No one is comparing him to Kevu's trophy cabinet. We're comparing his trajectory to the expectations. Keisner was written off before the season — 19th in 2024, 17th in 2025. Now he's P6 in a factory Audi in its debut year. That's not a fluke; that's a pattern. You don't accidentally outqualify your teammate, score in every feature race, and win Driver of the Day unless you belong. The chaos argument works once. Twice? Three times? At some point you have to admit the driver is making his own luck.

replies.author: RealityCheck
replies.text: Both of you are right in different ways. quarter_defender is correct that the sample size is small and chaos has helped. FourRingsOrBust is correct that Keisner's racecraft has been genuinely excellent. The answer is probably in the middle: enjoy the story, but hold off on the 'future champion' talk until we see a clean weekend where he qualifies well and finishes well without a dozen DNFs ahead of him. Suzuka is that test. If Keisner starts in the top ten and finishes in the top five on merit, the hype is real. If he's P15 and invisible, quarter_defender gets to say 'I told you so.' That's how it works.

replies.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
replies.text: Or we could just let people enjoy a good story without turning every driver into a spreadsheet. Keisner is fun. Audi is fun. Not everything needs a 17-page evidence dossier. Sometimes a P5 from P20 is just cool. Let the man cook.

comments.author: AudiRising
comments.text: From last on the grid to P5. In a manufacturer team that didn't exist two years ago. Keisner has now outscored his entire 2024 tally in two weekends. This isn't a plucky underdog story anymore — this is a legitimate points-scoring operation. The Audi PU is strong, the chassis is improving, and Keisner is driving like a man with nothing to lose and everything to prove. China showed the paddock that this project is for real.

replies.author: FourRingsOrBust
replies.text: The most impressive part? He didn't put a wheel wrong. No penalties, no contact, just clean overtakes and tyre preservation. After the Q1 knockout, a lesser driver would have overdriven. Keisner kept his head and picked off cars one by one. That's maturity far beyond his years.

comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: Keisner finished 19th in 2024 with Alpine, 17th last year with Sauber, and now he's dragging an Audi into the top five. The career arc is genuinely wild. Two sprint podiums and a P5 in the race — he's scored more points in two weekends than many midfield drivers will all season. If Audi can sort out their pit stops, he's a regular top-eight threat.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: The jump from 67 points in a full 2025 season to 16 already this year shows how much of a difference a works team can make. Keisner was always quick but had no platform. Now he has one, and the results are immediate.

comments.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
comments.text: Quiet stat: Keisner finished ahead of both Mercedes, both Williams, both Haas, and one Ferrari in China. From P20. If Audi figure out Saturday, he's a podium regular.

replies.author: MercEngineer
replies.text: Mercedes had a shocker in China, but credit where it's due — Keisner was faster than us on Sunday. That's not something I expected to say in round two.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: Audi's first season as a full works team and their driver is P4 in the championship after two rounds. Above Roy, above Zwart, above Springfield. Absolutely nobody predicted this in pre-season. The Keisner redemption arc is my favourite storyline of 2026.

comments.author: CachinhoPressure
comments.text: Keisner has 16 points. Cachinho has zero. One drives for Audi in their debut year, the other drives for Ferrari in a race-winning car. Just saying.

replies.author: scuderia_suffering
replies.text: To be fair, Keisner didn't have the entire paddock dissecting his every move. The pressure at Ferrari is different. But yes, the numbers are brutal. Cachinho needs Suzuka to go perfectly.

comments.author: FaktLap1Legend
comments.text: Keisner's P5 was impressive, but let's not forget Sam Fakt finished P5... in the list of drivers who retired on lap one. The GOAT knows when to conserve energy. China was just a tactical reset.

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: Sam Fakt has zero points, zero completed laps in two races, and you're still on this. The dedication is almost admirable. Almost.

comments.author: Zwart_Horse_187
comments.text: Keisner's result highlights Mercedes' problems. Our car was fast in Melbourne but nowhere in China. Meanwhile an Audi — an Audi! — charged through the field to finish fifth. If the team can't get a grip on the car's inconsistency, Keisner might finish ahead of Zwart in the standings.

replies.author: SilverArrowsFan
replies.text: It's one race. China has never been kind to Mercedes. Suzuka is a proper track that suits our philosophy. Let's see where Keisner is after Japan before we start panicking.

comments.author: Kevu2Wright
comments.text: Keisner's charge from the back was great, but he's benefiting from a chaotic start to the season. Once the grid stabilises and the faster cars sort their issues, those recovery drives will be harder. Impressive, but let's not crown him yet.

replies.author: AudiRising
replies.text: Nobody's crowning him. But when you outperform the car's expected level consistently, it's not just chaos. It's talent.
